title: What a Game Is (and the Core Genres)
html:
A game is a system, not a story

Strip away the art and the plot and every video game reduces to the same skeleton: a set of rules, one or more goals, and feedback that tells the player how they are doing. The player acts, the system reacts, the player adjusts. That tight cycle of action and reaction is the beating heart of every title from Tetris to Elden Ring.

  • Rules define what is possible: gravity pulls Mario down, blocks fall in Tetris, you can carry two weapons in Halo.
  • Goals give the rules a point: survive, score, reach the exit, defeat the boss. Goals stack from moment-to-moment (dodge that bullet) up through session (clear the level) to meta (finish the campaign).
  • Feedback closes the loop: a score ticks up, an enemy flashes red, a controller rumbles. Without feedback the player is acting blind.

The core loop

The most useful lens for any game is its core loop: the smallest repeating chunk of play. In Doom it is see enemy, aim, shoot, collect ammo, repeat. In Stardew Valley it is plant, water, wait, harvest, sell, buy more seeds. If the core loop is fun the game has a chance. If it is boring, no amount of story or graphics will save it. You will spend most of your design time tuning this loop.

Find the verb.
Every game has a dominant verb: jump, shoot, build, match, drive, talk. Naming it instantly clarifies the genre and what you must make feel good.

Genres and sub-genres

Genres are shorthand for a game's dominant mechanics and camera. They are not rigid boxes (modern games blend freely) but they help you communicate and find reference material.

GenreDominant verbExamples
PlatformerJump / traverseSuper Mario Bros, Celeste, Hollow Knight
Shooter (FPS/TPS)Aim / shootDoom, Halo, Fortnite
RPGGrow / decideFinal Fantasy, The Witcher 3, Disco Elysium
Strategy (RTS/TBS)Plan / commandStarCraft, Civilization, XCOM
PuzzleSolve / arrangeTetris, Portal, Baba Is You
Simulation / ManagementBuild / optimizeThe Sims, Factorio, Cities: Skylines
FightingTime / comboStreet Fighter, Tekken, Smash Bros
RacingDrive / lineMario Kart, Forza, Trackmania
Survival / SandboxGather / craftMinecraft, Valheim, Rust

Sub-genres refine these: a Metroidvania is a platformer with an interconnected, ability-gated map; a roguelike is a dungeon crawler with permadeath and procedural runs; a MOBA is a strategy/action hybrid. When you pitch a project, the fastest way to be understood is it is an X with a twist of Y.

Why this matters before you touch an engine

Choosing a genre early constrains every technical decision that follows: a platformer needs precise collision and a fixed-timestep feel; an RPG needs save systems and dialogue trees; an RTS needs pathfinding for hundreds of units. The genre tells you which of the systems in this Foundations part you must master first.

🎮 Build This: Three-Game Teardown

Train your eye to see the system behind the surface.

  1. Pick three games you love from different genres.
  2. For each write one line: Core loop (the smallest repeating action), Goal (what victory means), Genre + sub-genre.
  3. Underline the dominant verb in each core loop.
  4. Note one piece of feedback each game gives for a successful action.

title: The Game Loop, Frames & Delta Time
html:
The loop that never stops

A game is software that redraws itself many times per second while reacting to input. The structure that makes this happen is the game loop: a tight cycle that runs as fast as it can (or as fast as the display allows) for the entire time the game is open.

game-loop (pseudocode)
while running: input = pollInput() // what is the player doing? update(input, dt) // advance the simulation render() // draw the new state to screen

One pass through that cycle is a frame. The rate at which frames complete is FPS (frames per second). 60 FPS means the loop ran 60 times in one second, so each frame had about 16.7 milliseconds to finish all three steps. Miss that budget and the game stutters.

Why delta time exists

Different machines run the loop at different speeds. A 144Hz monitor runs the loop 144 times per second; a tired laptop might manage 40. If you move a player position += 5 every frame, the fast machine sends the player flying and the slow one crawls. The fix is delta time (dt): the number of seconds the previous frame actually took. Multiply all motion by dt and movement becomes framerate-independent: five units per second, not per frame.

The classic bug.
Forgetting to multiply by dt is the number-one beginner mistake. The game feels fine on your machine and is unplayable on everyone else's. If something moves by speed, it must be speed * dt.

Fixed vs variable timestep

There are two ways to feed time into update, and most games use both:

Variable timestepFixed timestep
dt each frameWhatever the frame tookAlways the same (e.g. 1/60s)
Good forRendering, camera, UI, simple movementPhysics, networking, deterministic logic
RiskLarge dt tunnels through walls; physics jittersMust be decoupled from render rate

Physics engines hate variable timesteps: a sudden long frame can teleport a fast object straight through a thin wall (tunneling), and integration becomes inaccurate. The professional solution is the accumulator pattern: render as fast as you can, but step physics in fixed chunks.

fixed-accumulator (pseudocode)
const STEP = 1.0 / 60.0 // fixed physics step, 60Hz let accumulator = 0 let last = now() while running: let current = now() let frameTime = current - last last = current accumulator += frameTime while accumulator >= STEP: // catch up in fixed slices physicsUpdate(STEP) accumulator -= STEP const alpha = accumulator / STEP // leftover 0..1 render(alpha) // interpolate for smoothness

The inner while runs physics zero, one, or several times depending on how long the frame took, keeping the simulation deterministic. The alpha value lets you interpolate the rendered position between the last two physics states so motion stays buttery. This is the model Unity exposes as FixedUpdate vs Update, and Godot as _physics_process vs _process.

Rule of thumb.
Put movement that must be deterministic or collide accurately in the fixed step. Put camera, animation blending, and UI in the variable step and multiply by dt.
🎮 Build This: Delta-Time Loop on Paper

Write a loop that moves an object framerate-independently, then add fixed physics.

  1. Write pseudocode for a loop that computes dt in seconds and does x += speed * dt.
  2. Trace it for two frames: one 16ms, one 33ms. Confirm the object moves the same distance per second in both.
  3. Wrap the physics in a fixed-timestep accumulator using the pattern above.

title: Coordinates, Vectors & Transforms
html:
Where things are: coordinate systems

Every object in a game has a position expressed in a coordinate system. In 2D you have an X axis (horizontal) and a Y axis (vertical); in 3D you add Z (depth). The catch that trips up beginners: engines disagree on which way is up and which way Y points.

System+Y directionUp axis (3D)
Most 2D canvases / screensDown—
Unity (3D)UpY, left-handed
Godot 2DDown—
Godot 3D / OpenGLUpY, right-handed

You will also hear world space (position in the whole level), local space (position relative to a parent), and screen space (pixel position after the camera projects the world). A child at local (0,1) sits one unit above its parent no matter where the parent moves.

Vectors: the workhorse of game math

A vector is just an ordered list of numbers ((x, y) in 2D) but it does double duty as both a position and a direction with magnitude. A handful of operations cover 90% of gameplay code:

  • Add: a + b = (ax+bx, ay+by). Moving by a velocity is pos + vel*dt.
  • Scale: a * s = (ax*s, ay*s). Doubles or halves a vector's length.
  • Magnitude (length): |a| = sqrt(ax*ax + ay*ay) via Pythagoras. The distance from the origin.
  • Normalize: a / |a| gives a unit vector (length 1): pure direction, no speed. Essential before applying a speed.
  • Dot product: a.b = ax*bx + ay*by, equal to |a||b|cos(theta). Positive means the vectors point the same way, zero means perpendicular, negative means opposing. Use it to check if an enemy is in front of you.
move-toward (pseudocode)
// move mover toward target at 5 units/sec let dir = target - mover // vector from mover to target let dist = length(dir) if dist > 0.001: dir = dir / dist // normalize -> unit direction mover = mover + dir * 5 * dt // step along it, dt-scaled
Normalize before scaling.
Diagonal movement is the classic gotcha: pressing right+up gives (1,1) which has length 1.41, so the player moves 41% faster diagonally. Normalize the input vector first.

A little trig

To turn an angle into a direction use dir = (cos(angle), sin(angle)). To get the angle of a vector use angle = atan2(y, x) (always atan2, never plain atan, because it handles all four quadrants). This is how you aim a turret at the mouse or rotate a sprite to face its velocity.

Transforms: position, rotation, scale

A transform bundles an object's position, rotation, and scale. Engines store it as a matrix, but you rarely touch the math directly: you set transform.position, transform.rotation, transform.scale and the engine composes them. The key idea is the hierarchy: a child's final world transform is its local transform multiplied by its parent's. Move a car and its wheels (children) come along; rotate the car and the wheels orbit with it. This parenting is how you build a hand attached to an arm attached to a body.

🎮 Build This: Direction & Step on Paper

Compute a real movement step by hand.

  1. Let A = (2, 1) and B = (6, 4). Compute the vector B - A.
  2. Find its length, then divide to get the normalized direction.
  3. The mover travels at 5 units/sec. Compute the new position after 0.2s: A + dir * 5 * 0.2.
  4. Check your work: the step length should be exactly 1.0 unit.

title: Sprites, Textures, Atlases, Meshes & Materials
html:
2D assets

In 2D games the visible objects are sprites: flat images drawn on screen, usually with transparency. The raw image data (the grid of colored pixels stored in memory or on the GPU) is a texture. A sprite is essentially draw this texture, or part of it, at this position, rotation and scale.

Textures have a few properties you must understand:

  • Resolution: width x height in pixels. GPUs historically preferred power-of-two sizes (256, 512, 1024); modern hardware is flexible but POT still helps with mipmaps and compression.
  • Filtering: nearest-neighbor keeps pixels crisp (essential for pixel art); bilinear/linear smooths them (good for high-res art). Pick wrong and your crisp pixel art turns into a blurry mess.
  • Alpha channel: the transparency. PNG carries it; JPEG does not.

Atlases: why you pack sprites together

Switching the active texture is one of the most expensive things a GPU does, and each switch can force a separate draw call. A texture atlas (also called a sprite sheet) packs many sprites into one big image so the GPU binds it once and draws everything from it. This is the foundation of batching (Lesson 11).

To draw one sprite from an atlas you need its UV coordinates: a rectangle inside the texture expressed from 0 to 1, where (0,0) is one corner and (1,1) the opposite. If a 64px frame sits at pixel (128, 0) in a 256x256 atlas, its UVs run from (0.5, 0.0) to (0.75, 0.25).

uv-from-pixels (pseudocode)
// frame rect in pixels -> UV rect (0..1) u0 = frameX / atlasWidth v0 = frameY / atlasHeight u1 = (frameX + frameW) / atlasWidth v1 = (frameY + frameH) / atlasHeight
Mind the bleed.
Leave 1-2 px of padding between atlas frames or bilinear filtering will sample neighboring pixels and you will see thin colored seams. Tools like TexturePacker add this extrude automatically.

3D assets

3D objects are built from meshes: collections of vertices (points in 3D space) joined into triangles (the only shape GPUs draw). A cube has 8 vertices and 12 triangles. Each vertex carries data beyond position: a normal (which way the surface faces, for lighting) and UVs (where to sample the texture, exactly like 2D).

A model is the broader asset: usually a mesh plus its materials, and often a skeleton and animations (Lesson 8). Files like .glTF/.glb, .fbx, and .obj store models. glTF is the modern open standard and the safest default.

Materials and shaders

A material describes how a surface looks: which textures it uses and how it responds to light. Modern engines use PBR (physically based rendering) materials with maps like:

MapControls
Albedo / Base ColorThe raw surface color
NormalFake surface bumps without extra geometry
MetallicHow metal-like the surface is
RoughnessHow sharp or blurry reflections are
Ambient OcclusionSoft contact shadows in crevices

Under the hood a material runs a shader: a small GPU program (covered in Lesson 11) that computes the final pixel color. For now the mental model is: mesh = shape, material = look, texture = the image data the material samples.

🎮 Build This: Walk-Cycle Atlas Layout

Design a sprite atlas and compute one frame's UVs.

  1. Sketch a 4-frame walk cycle laid out horizontally in a 256x64 atlas (each frame 64x64).
  2. Label the pixel position (x,y,w,h) of each frame.
  3. Pick frame 3 and compute its UV rect using the formula above.
  4. Mark where you would add padding to avoid bleed.

title: The Rendering Pipeline, Cameras & Viewports
html:
From world to pixels

Rendering is the process of turning your game's data (positions, meshes, textures) into the colored pixels on screen. Every frame the GPU runs your geometry through a pipeline of transformations. You will not write most of it by hand, but understanding the stages explains nearly every camera and resolution problem you will ever debug.

  1. Model space: vertices as the artist made them, centered on the object's origin.
  2. World transform: place the object in the level (its transform from Lesson 3). Now in world space.
  3. View transform: move everything so the camera sits at the origin looking down one axis. Now in view space.
  4. Projection transform: squash 3D into a 2D image, either perspective (distant things shrink) or orthographic (no shrink, used for 2D and isometric). Now in clip space.
  5. Clipping and perspective divide: throw away anything outside the view, divide by depth. Now in normalized device coordinates, a -1..1 cube.
  6. Viewport transform: map that cube onto the actual pixel rectangle on screen. Now in screen space.
  7. Rasterization and fragment shading: fill triangles with pixels, run the fragment shader per pixel to compute color, write to the framebuffer.
The acronym.
The matrix chain is often called MVP: Model x View x Projection. A vertex's clip position is MVP * vertexPosition.

Cameras

A camera is just an object whose transform defines the view stage, plus a choice of projection. Two flavors:

  • Orthographic: parallel projection, no perspective. The default for 2D games and for isometric/2.5D. You set an orthographic size (how many world units tall the view is).
  • Perspective: objects shrink with distance, governed by a field of view (FOV) angle. Higher FOV sees more but distorts edges; 60-90 degrees is typical. You also set a near and far clip plane: anything closer than near or farther than far is not drawn.

Viewport and resolution

The viewport is the rectangle of the window the camera draws into (you can have several; split-screen is two viewports). Resolution is how many pixels that rectangle contains. The hard part is aspect ratio: players have 16:9 monitors, ultrawides, and phones. If you design for one and ignore the rest, the image stretches or crops badly.

ModeBehaviorUse when
Stretch / scaleFill the window, may distortRarely; looks wrong on odd ratios
Keep aspect (letterbox)Add black bars, never distortFixed-camera arcade / pixel-art games
ExpandShow more world on wider screensMost 3D games and modern 2D
Pixel-art shimmer.
If your camera or sprite positions land on fractional pixels, pixel art shimmers and tears. Use an integer scale factor and snap the camera to whole pixels, or render to a low-res target and upscale it whole.

Many engines distinguish the render resolution (how many pixels you draw) from the window size (how big it appears). Rendering at a lower internal resolution and upscaling is a key performance lever, especially on consoles and phones.

🎮 Build This: Diagram the Pipeline

Cement the world-to-pixels path by drawing it.

  1. Draw seven boxes left to right: model, world, view, clip, NDC, screen, pixels.
  2. Label which transform sits between each pair (M, V, P, divide, viewport).
  3. Mark where the camera defines the View and Projection stages.
  4. Mark where the viewport rectangle and resolution decide the final pixel mapping.

title: Reading Input: Keyboard, Mouse, Gamepad & Touch
html:
Devices give raw signals; games want actions

Input devices report low-level events: key W is down, mouse moved to (812, 410), gamepad left stick is at (-0.7, 0.2), finger touched at (200, 600). Your game does not care about W; it cares about move forward. The professional pattern is an action map (also called an input map or bindings) that translates raw device signals into named game actions. This is the single most important idea in this lesson.

Never check device keys in gameplay code.
Do not write if key W is down: moveForward(). Write if action("Forward") and bind Forward to W, the up arrow, the left stick, and a touch button. This is what makes rebinding, controller support, and accessibility possible later.

Polling vs event-driven

There are two ways to read input, and most games use both:

PollingEvent-driven
HowAsk each frame: is Jump held?The system calls you when something changes
Good forContinuous state: movement, aiming, held buttonsDiscrete moments: a click, a key press, text entry
RiskCan miss a press+release inside one frameEvents can queue up or arrive out of loop order

The crucial distinction for buttons is three states, not one:

  • Pressed (this frame only): the button went from up to down. Use for jump, fire, menu select: anything that should happen once per press.
  • Held / Down: the button is currently down. Use for movement, holding a charge.
  • Released (this frame only): went from down to up. Use for charged shots or jump-height cutoff.
The double-jump bug.
If you trigger jump on held instead of pressed, the player jumps every frame the button is down, dozens of times. Always gate one-shot actions on the pressed edge.

Per-device gotchas

  • Keyboard/mouse: mouse position is in screen space; you must unproject it through the camera to get a world position. Mouse delta (movement) is better for first-person look than absolute position.
  • Gamepad: analog sticks report a continuous vector and never quite rest at zero. Apply a dead zone (ignore magnitudes below ~0.15) or the character drifts. Triggers are analog (0..1), not buttons.
  • Touch: there is no hover and no precise pointer; design for fat fingers, support multi-touch (a virtual stick plus a button), and remember a tap is a touch-down + touch-up close together in time and space.
action-map (pseudocode)
actions = { "Jump": { keys:[SPACE, W], pad:[BUTTON_A], touch:["btnJump"] }, "MoveX": { keys:[A,-1, D,+1], pad:[LSTICK_X], touch:["vstickX"] } } // each frame, build a clean action state function update(): moveX = readAxis("MoveX") // -1..1, dead-zoned if pressed("Jump"): doJump() // edge, fires once
🎮 Build This: Platformer Action Map

Design bindings for jump, move, and dash across three device types.

  1. Make a table with rows Move, Jump, Dash and columns Keyboard, Gamepad, Touch.
  2. Fill each cell with the concrete binding (e.g. Move = A/D, left stick, virtual stick).
  3. Mark which actions read the pressed edge vs continuous held state.
  4. Note one dead-zone or fat-finger consideration for the gamepad and touch columns.

title: Collision Detection & Physics Basics
html:
Detecting that two things touch

Collision detection answers do these two shapes overlap? Games use simplified collider shapes, not the full visual mesh, because cheap shapes test thousands of times per frame. The three you must know:

AABB (Axis-Aligned Bounding Box)

A non-rotated rectangle defined by min and max corners. Overlap is true only if the boxes overlap on both axes: fast and the workhorse of 2D and broad-phase 3D.

aabb-overlap (pseudocode)
function aabbOverlap(a, b): return a.minX < b.maxX and a.maxX > b.minX and a.minY < b.maxY and a.maxY > b.minY // all four must be true; one separating axis = no hit

Circle / sphere

Two circles overlap when the distance between their centers is less than the sum of their radii. To skip the square root, compare squared distances:

circle-overlap (pseudocode)
dx = b.x - a.x; dy = b.y - a.y rSum = a.r + b.r return (dx*dx + dy*dy) < (rSum*rSum) // cheaper than sqrt

Raycast

A ray is an origin plus a direction; a raycast finds the first collider it hits and where. This is how you do line-of-sight (can the guard see the player?), shooting hitscan weapons, ground checks under a character's feet, and mouse-picking objects in 3D. The result usually returns the hit point, distance, and surface normal.

Broad phase vs narrow phase.
Engines first do a cheap broad-phase pass (often AABBs in a spatial grid or tree) to find possible pairs, then an expensive narrow-phase exact test only on those. You get this for free, but knowing it explains why fewer, larger colliders perform better.

Physics: making things move believably

A rigidbody is a collider with mass and velocity that the physics engine simulates. You generally do not set its position directly; you apply forces or impulses and let the integrator move it:

  • Force: applied over time (like thrust or gravity), changes velocity gradually. F = m * a.
  • Impulse: instant change in velocity (like a jump or an explosion kick).
  • Gravity: a constant downward acceleration applied every fixed step.
PropertyMeaning
MassResistance to force; heavier = harder to push
Restitution (bounciness)0 = no bounce (clay), 1 = perfect bounce (super ball)
FrictionResistance to sliding along a surface
DragResistance to moving through space (air/water)

Restitution drives bouncing. On impact the engine reflects the velocity component along the surface normal and scales it by restitution: a ball hitting the floor with vy = -10 and restitution 0.8 leaves with vy = +8. Each bounce keeps 80%, so it gradually settles, exactly what you would expect.

Triggers vs solid colliders

A solid collider physically stops things: the player cannot walk through a wall. A trigger (sensor) detects overlap but lets objects pass through, firing a callback like onTriggerEnter. Use triggers for pickups, checkpoints, damage zones, and player entered the boss arena events.

Move rigidbodies in the fixed step.
Apply forces and set velocity in FixedUpdate/_physics_process, not in the render frame. Mixing physics with variable timestep (Lesson 2) causes jitter and tunneling.
🎮 Build This: AABB Test & Bouncing Ball

Write the overlap test and reason about restitution.

  1. Write the boolean aabbOverlap(a, b) from scratch without peeking.
  2. Test it on two boxes you draw: one overlapping pair, one separated pair.
  3. Sketch a ball falling and bouncing with restitution 0.7. Mark the velocity right before and right after each of the first three bounces.
  4. Note at which restitution value the ball would never stop bouncing.

title: Animation: Frames, Skeletons & Easing
html:
Three ways to make things move

Animation is changing a property over time. Games use three distinct techniques, and choosing the right one for a given motion is the skill this lesson builds.

1. Frame-based (sprite) animation

The oldest and simplest: flip through a sequence of pre-drawn images, like a flipbook. A walk cycle might be 8 frames played at 12 frames per second. You store the frames in an atlas (Lesson 4) and advance an index on a timer.

sprite-anim (pseudocode)
timer += dt if timer >= 1.0 / fps: timer -= 1.0 / fps frame = (frame + 1) % frameCount // loop the cycle drawSprite(atlas, frames[frame], pos)

Use it for: pixel-art and 2D games, particle-like effects, anything where hand-drawn frames give the look you want. Cost: memory (every frame is a separate image) and inflexibility (you cannot retarget or blend).

2. Skeletal animation

A skeleton (rig) is a hierarchy of bones, really just transforms parented to each other (Lesson 3). The mesh's vertices are skinned to the bones with weights, so when a bone rotates the nearby vertices follow. Animators store keyframes of bone rotations over time, and the engine interpolates between them.

  • Tiny storage: you save bone rotations, not whole images.
  • Blending: smoothly transition from walk to run, or layer an upper-body aim onto a lower-body run.
  • Inverse kinematics (IK): pin a foot to the ground or a hand to a ledge and let the engine solve the joint angles.
  • Retargeting: reuse one animation on many characters sharing a skeleton.
Blend trees and state machines.
Modern engines drive skeletal animation with an animation state machine (compare Lesson 10): states like Idle/Walk/Run/Jump with transition conditions, and blend trees that mix clips by a parameter such as speed.

3. Tweening and easing

A tween (in-between) animates a single property from a start value to an end value over a duration: a menu sliding in, a health bar filling, a chest popping open. You compute a normalized time t from 0 to 1, pass it through an easing function, and interpolate.

Linear motion (constant speed) looks robotic. Easing curves make it feel alive:

EasingFeelUse for
LinearMechanical, constantConveyor belts, loading bars
Ease-outFast start, slow stopUI arriving, things settling
Ease-inSlow start, fast endObjects accelerating away
Ease-in-outSmooth both endsCamera moves, transitions
Back / elastic / bounceOvershoots / springsJuicy pops, pickups, buttons
tween-easeout (pseudocode)
elapsed += dt t = clamp(elapsed / duration, 0, 1) e = 1 - (1 - t) * (1 - t) // ease-out quad: fast start, slow stop value = lerp(start, end, e) // lerp(a,b,e) = a + (b-a)*e
Match technique to motion.
Discrete, hand-drawn looks use frame-based. Articulated characters use skeletal. A single property moving from A to B uses a tween with the right easing. Many games use all three at once.
🎮 Build This: Chest Pop Keyframes

Design a tween for a chest opening over 0.4 seconds.

  1. Choose the property to animate (the lid's rotation, say 0 to -110 degrees).
  2. Write keyframes: t=0 closed, t=0.4 open. Note the duration.
  3. Pick ease-out so the lid flings open then settles; write the eased t formula.
  4. Add a tiny back-easing overshoot at the end for extra juice and describe the visual effect.

title: Scenes, Entities, Components & ECS
html:
Scenes: the container for a slice of game

A scene (Unity), node tree (Godot), or level is a container holding all the objects active at one time: the player, enemies, the camera, lights, UI. Loading a new scene swaps one set of objects for another (main menu to gameplay, level 1 to level 2). Scenes are how you carve a big game into shippable, loadable chunks, and how you keep memory in check by unloading what you are not using.

The problem with deep inheritance

Early game code modeled objects with class inheritance: GameObject -> Character -> Enemy -> FlyingEnemy. This breaks fast. What about a flying enemy that can also be picked up? Or a destructible object that also heals? Inheritance forces a single rigid tree, and you end up with a god-class or duplicated code. The industry answer is composition over inheritance.

Composition over inheritance.
Do not ask what is this object? Ask what parts does it have? Build objects by attaching small, reusable components instead of deriving from a tall class hierarchy.

The entity/component pattern

An entity is a container with an identity but little behavior of its own. Components are small modules you attach to it, each owning one concern. A player is not a Player class; it is an entity with a Transform, a Sprite, a Rigidbody, a Health, and a PlayerInput component. This is the model behind Unity's GameObject + MonoBehaviour and Godot's node composition.

entity-composition (pseudocode)
player = Entity() player.add(Transform(pos)) player.add(Sprite("hero.png")) player.add(Rigidbody(mass=1)) player.add(Health(max=100)) player.add(PlayerInput()) // an enemy reuses the same parts, swaps the input enemy = Entity() enemy.add(Transform(pos)); enemy.add(Sprite("orc.png")) enemy.add(Rigidbody(mass=2)); enemy.add(Health(max=40)) enemy.add(AIBrain())

From components to ECS

Classic entity/component still puts logic inside each component. Full ECS (Entity Component System) takes the idea further and is a key buzzword to understand:

  • Entity: just an ID, a number. No data, no logic.
  • Component: pure data, no behavior (a Position is just x,y; a Velocity is just dx,dy).
  • System: pure logic that runs over every entity having a specific set of components. A MovementSystem reads all entities with Position + Velocity and updates them.

This separation has two payoffs. First, it is flexible: behavior is decided by which components an entity has, composable at runtime. Second, and the real reason AAA and simulation-heavy games adopt it, is performance: storing components of the same type contiguously in memory (data-oriented design) lets a system rip through thousands of them with cache-friendly, parallelizable loops. Unity's DOTS and engines like Bevy are built on this.

When to usePattern
Most games, small/medium teamsEntity + components with behavior (GameObject/Node)
Thousands of similar entities, perf-critical simsFull data-oriented ECS
Do not cargo-cult ECS.
Full ECS adds boilerplate and indirection. For a platformer or a puzzle game the built-in component model is simpler and plenty fast. Reach for full ECS when profiling shows you need it, or when entity counts are huge.
🎮 Build This: Component Inventory

Model three objects with the entity/component pattern.

  1. List the components for a Player, an Enemy, and a Pickup.
  2. Mark which components are shared (e.g. Transform, Sprite) and which are unique.
  3. Name three systems (e.g. Movement, Render, Collision) and note which components each reads.
  4. State whether this game needs full ECS or the basic component model, and why.

title: Game States & Finite State Machines
html:
Why state machines are everywhere

A finite state machine (FSM) is the most-used pattern in all of game development. The idea: at any moment the system is in exactly one of a finite set of states, and it moves between them along defined transitions triggered by events or conditions. FSMs tame complexity by making every behavior explicit and every transition deliberate.

You will use FSMs at two scales:

  • Game flow: Menu to Play to Pause to GameOver. Only one is active; each owns its own update and draw.
  • Entity behavior: an enemy that is Patrolling, Chasing, Attacking, or Returning; a character that is Idle, Running, Jumping, Falling.

Anatomy of an FSM

Each state defines three things: what happens on enter (set up, play an animation), what happens every update (the ongoing behavior), and what happens on exit (clean up). Transitions live as conditions checked during update.

fsm (pseudocode)
state = MENU function update(dt): switch state: case MENU: if pressed("Start"): changeState(PLAY) case PLAY: updateGameplay(dt) if pressed("Pause"): changeState(PAUSE) if player.health <= 0: changeState(GAMEOVER) case PAUSE: if pressed("Pause"): changeState(PLAY) case GAMEOVER: if pressed("Confirm"): changeState(MENU) function changeState(next): onExit(state) // stop music, hide UI... state = next onEnter(state) // show UI, reset timers...
One state at a time.
The whole value of an FSM is the guarantee that exactly one state is active. If you find yourself wanting two states at once (e.g. paused-but-also-animating), that is a sign you need a stack of states or layered FSMs, not booleans scattered everywhere.

Game flow with a state stack

A plain FSM forgets where it came from. Pausing is the classic example: when you unpause you want to resume exactly the gameplay underneath, not rebuild it. A state stack (pushdown automaton) solves this: push Pause on top of Play, and pop it to return. The state below keeps its data and simply stops updating while it is covered.

Entity AI as an FSM

A guard enemy is a perfect FSM. Each state has clear behavior and clear exits:

StateBehaviorTransition out
PatrolWalk a fixed routeSees player -> Chase
ChaseMove toward playerIn attack range -> Attack; lost player -> Return
AttackStrike, play attack animPlayer leaves range -> Chase; player dead -> Patrol
ReturnWalk back to postReached post -> Patrol; sees player -> Chase

When FSMs grow past a dozen states with tangled transitions, teams graduate to behavior trees or hierarchical state machines (previewed in Lesson 11) which scale better. But the FSM is where every game AI conversation starts, and most enemies never need anything fancier.

🎮 Build This: Guard FSM Diagram

Draw a finite state machine for a patrolling guard.

  1. Draw four boxes: Patrol, Chase, Attack, Return.
  2. Draw arrows between them and label each with its trigger condition.
  3. Mark the starting state and what each state does on enter (e.g. Attack plays the swing animation).
  4. Add one note: which transition would you make use a state stack and why?

title: Tilemaps, Audio, AI, UI, Assets, Saving, Performance & Beyond
html:
The rest of the toolbox

You now have the core mental models. This lesson is a guided tour of the remaining systems every game uses, plus the production practices that turn ideas into shipped games. Treat it as a map: each topic gets a full part later, but knowing the vocabulary now means nothing surprises you.

Tilemaps and level design

A tilemap builds 2D levels from a grid of reusable tiles drawn from a tileset (one atlas). Instead of placing thousands of sprites, you paint a grid; the engine batches it into a handful of draw calls. Autotiling picks the right edge/corner tile automatically, and a separate collision layer marks which tiles are solid. Good level design teaches mechanics through space: introduce, then test, then combine.

Audio

Three layers: SFX (short one-shots like jumps and hits), music (looping tracks, sometimes adaptive), and spatial/positional audio (sounds that pan and attenuate by distance in 3D). Engines route everything through buses (mixer channels) so you can have Master, Music, SFX, and UI sliders, apply effects per bus, and duck the music when dialogue plays.

Game AI

Beyond the FSM (Lesson 10), the core AI toolkit is: A* pathfinding (find the shortest route across a grid or navmesh by exploring nodes ordered by cost-so-far plus a heuristic), steering behaviors (seek, flee, arrive, wander, flocking: smooth force-based movement), and behavior trees (a scalable tree of selectors and sequences that replaces sprawling FSMs in complex agents).

A* in one breath.
Keep an open set of frontier nodes; always expand the one with the lowest f = g + h (g = cost from start, h = estimated cost to goal). When you pop the goal, walk the parent links back to build the path.

UI and HUD

The HUD shows live game state (health, score, ammo, minimap); UI covers menus, inventories, and dialogs. UI is laid out with anchors and containers so it adapts to any resolution (tie this back to Lesson 5's aspect-ratio handling), and is usually drawn in screen space on top of the world.

Asset pipeline and formats

The asset pipeline imports source files (PSD, FBX, WAV) and processes them into engine-ready forms (compressed textures, atlases, audio banks). Know the common formats: PNG (lossless 2D + alpha), glTF/FBX (3D models), OGG/WAV (audio), and the engine's own scene/prefab formats.

Save systems and serialization

Serialization turns live objects into bytes you can store and later deserialize back. Saves come in flavors: JSON (human-readable, easy to debug), binary (compact, fast), and key-value stores for settings. Decide early what is save-worthy (progress, inventory, position) versus derived state you can rebuild, and version your save format so old saves still load.

Performance

TechniqueWhat it saves
Culling (frustum/occlusion)Skip drawing what the camera cannot see
Batching / instancingCombine draws sharing a material into one call
Object poolingReuse objects (bullets, enemies) instead of allocating, avoiding GC spikes
LODUse simpler meshes at distance
Profile before you optimize.
Use the engine's profiler to find the real bottleneck (CPU? GPU? memory?). Guessing wastes days; the slow thing is rarely what you assume.

Advanced topics in one paragraph each

Shaders: small programs (GLSL in OpenGL/Vulkan, HLSL in DirectX) running on the GPU per-vertex and per-pixel; they drive everything from water to outlines. Particles: thousands of tiny sprites/meshes spawned by emitters for fire, smoke, sparks. Networking: pick a model. lockstep (send inputs, simulate identically, great for RTS), or client-server with authority + client-side prediction (most action games). Procedural generation: build content from rules and noise (terrain, dungeons, loot). Juice / game-feel: the polish layer. screen shake, hit-stop, particles, easing, sound. that makes simple actions feel great.

Production, source control, and scope

Use git for code (and Git LFS or Perforce for big binary assets). A simple branching plan: main stays shippable, you branch feature/* per task and merge back via review. Scoping is the skill that ships games: list your features, then aggressively cut to a vertical slice you can finish. A maintained cut-list (nice-to-haves you will drop under pressure) is your best friend.

🎮 Build This: One-Page Scope Doc

Scope a tiny game so it is actually finishable.

  1. Pick a tiny game idea and write its core loop in one sentence (callback to Lesson 1).
  2. List the systems it needs from this part (e.g. tilemap, input map, FSM, SFX, save).
  3. Write a feature cut-list: 3 must-haves and 3 nice-to-haves you will drop first.
  4. Sketch a git branching plan: main plus the feature branches and how they merge.

title: Choose Your Path: Pick One Track
html:
You've got the fundamentals — now go deep on ONE path

Everything in Part 00 — the game loop, vectors, sprites, collision, state machines — is true everywhere. Now comes the choice that shapes the rest of this course: pick one path and stay with it. You do not need to do all of them. Real skill comes from building many games in one tool, not one game in many tools.

The plan.
Choose ONE path below. Its track is a single connected journey: each lesson's "🎮 Build This" either extends the game you built last time or starts a brand-new genre. By the end you'll have a portfolio of several different-genre games — all in your one path.

Your six paths

A path is either a game engine (a mature toolset that handles the heavy lifting) or a language from scratch (you write the engine yourself with a light library — the deepest understanding and the most control). Pick the one that fits your goals.

Game engines

PathLanguageChoose it if you want…
Godot · Part 01GDScriptFree & lightweight, superb 2D, the gentlest start. A great first engine.
Unity · Part 02C#The most jobs and tutorials, strong 2D and 3D. The safe all-rounder.
Unreal · Part 03Blueprints + C++Cutting-edge 3D and AAA fidelity, visual scripting. Pick it if 3D excites you.

From scratch — no engine

PathLibraryChoose it if you want…
C++ · Part 04raylib / SFMLMaximum performance and control — and the language most professional engines are built in.
Python · Part 05PygameThe simplest language to learn the loop from scratch — fast to write, easy to read.
JavaScript · Part 06HTML5 CanvasGames that run instantly in any browser with zero install — the easiest to share.

There is no wrong choice — any path can build any genre. New to all of this? Start with Godot or Unity (engines) or Python (from scratch). Want games on the web fast? JavaScript. Chasing performance or 3D? C++ or Unreal.

The genres you'll build

Whichever path you choose, the track walks you across genres as your skills grow — for example a first toy → an arcade game (Pong/Breakout) → a platformer → a top-down shooter → a survival or tower-defense game → a puzzle game → an endless runner → a polished capstone. Each one teaches new skills and a new genre's design.

The rest applies to everyone

Parts 07–09 (Blender, Game Audio, Ship It) work no matter which path you chose — bring your portfolio games here to add 3D art, sound, and a real release.

🎮 Build This: Plan — Your Portfolio

No code yet — set your direction.

  1. Pick your ONE path — an engine (Godot/Unity/Unreal) or a language (C++/Python/JavaScript) — and commit to it for the whole track.
  2. Install it and open a blank project so you're ready for Lesson 1 of your chosen part.
  3. Write down 5–6 game genres you want to have built by the end (arcade, platformer, shooter, puzzle, runner…) — that's your portfolio target.
